text: I'm not sure I've seen a more perfectly constructed film than Rosemary's Baby. There are many films that are more artfully or cinematically constructed, but rewatching it we were absolutely struck by how there is zero fat on this film. Every scene, every shot, every performance tone and note seem to work in a completely tireless movie. The film is like a clockwork.
label: hot_take

text: My favorite thing about Rosemary's Baby is that there is no specific twist moment where everything changes. It is a completely smooth gradual burn, nothing obvious, little by little, until she's in the phone booth making the call. A lesser film would have had a scene where the neighbors turn obviously bad. But this film lets you just feel the creeping dread with her, not really understanding what's happening but instinctively knowing it's dangerous.
label: analysis

text: Rosemary's Baby is a very good movie, but I read the book after watching it and the book is better. They are 98% similar but that 2% is what makes the book better. The film was difficult to tell how much time had passed, which the book makes explicit.
label: reaction

text: Nolan's recent films all seem to be converging on the same visual style. Dunkirk, Tenet, Oppenheimer and now The Odyssey all have the same earthy, brown-grey, tactile large-format look. Villeneuve feels very different — Enemy looks nothing like Prisoners, Prisoners looks nothing like Sicario. With Nolan it feels like how do I make this look like a Nolan film? With Villeneuve it feels like what should this particular story look like?
label: analysis

text: Nolan's visual language has always been consistent and it leans heavily on his cinematographer. His work with Wally Pfister looked similar across films. He switched to Hoyte van Hoytema with Interstellar and there was a noticeable shift. Villeneuve swaps cinematographers constantly — he's worked with Roger Deakins, Bradford Young, Greig Fraser, and Linus Sandgren. The idea of a director and DP honing a consistent visual language is by no means unusual.
label: analysis

text: The center-framing trend needs to stop. I believe it started with Fury Road where it was a smart stylistic decision because it helped contain the chaos. But now so many films use it for no good reason. It removes an entire element of the language of cinema: the guiding of the audience's eye. Part of this comes from a desire to make films social media-friendly and easy to convert into vertical trailers.
label: analysis

text: I'm completely tired of so many new horror movies looking the same. They all do these Shining ripoff things, center frame everything, the same colors, the same pacing, the same scores, the same actors, even the same themes about grief and trauma. I'm bored to death.
label: hot_take

text: M. Night Shyamalan's Devil uses center-framing appropriately as a metaphor for the devil looking up at Heaven. Midsommar also uses it effectively when traveling to the festival, to denote that the narrative is no longer in the characters' control. But by the time Smile does it, it's the millionth horror film to use it and the effect is muted because it's not necessary to the story.
label: analysis

text: I spent most of my film-watching life prioritising the canon, but somewhere in my late 30s I fell headlong into genre films, exploitation cinema, midnight movies. I started to see the lineage, the fusion and the influences running in both directions. One feeding the other and back again. It made everything else make more sense.
label: reaction

text: Film, like any other tradition of human activity, progresses historically through a sequence of references and influences. If someone is incapable of simultaneously acknowledging this is an essential film for understanding how film has developed and I don't like this film then they have some maturing to do. The way to avoid the reactionary aspect of canon-building is to be open to broadening its scope, not to disregard it altogether.
label: analysis

text: I have my own evolving personal canon full of arthouse stuff. But there are genre films I genuinely think are masterful and yet I don't include them, and I'm not sure why. It just feels wrong to put Die Hard and Wild Strawberries on the same list. But honestly I can't argue that there is less talent and artistry involved in Die Hard than Wild Strawberries. They're both masterpieces in my eyes.
label: hot_take

text: Texas Chainsaw Massacre bridges arthouse and grindhouse. Sci-fi used to be considered low-brow before 2001 came out and opinions shifted. Fury Road is a work of art with what it does with the action genre. Watch what you are curious about, not what you think you're supposed to watch.
label: hot_take

text: I'm a huge Spielberg fan but Disclosure Day felt closer to AI in quality. The pacing and story felt quite rushed — genuinely more like a series of moments than a true narrative. Nothing felt very developed. The acting was largely good and I liked the camera work, but in its entirety it was a mediocre film.
label: reaction

text: The trailer promised something emotionally engaging but the final product felt surprisingly uneven. The pacing was one of the biggest issues — important things seemed rushed while other scenes added no depth. I struggled to connect with the characters because the film assumed investment before properly earning it.
label: reaction

text: As a Gen Xer who grew up watching all of the Spielberg gems, it's hard to see such a lazy effort.
label: hot_take

text: I was so excited from the trailers but left feeling like I spent 2.5 hours in my seat. You do not get attached to the characters at all. It really feels like you decided to watch a series from season 2, skipping season 1. The ending was anticlimactic — I really expected more twists. I would give this movie 4 out of 10.
label: reaction

text: I am genuinely shocked at how much of a mess this was. The script was awful, the character work was awful, the message haphazardly put together. The plot made no sense and villain motivations were void. The ending was pointless with no payoff for the hundreds of dead air scenes and endless exposition.
label: hot_take

text: Banger of a film, a perfect bookend to Spielberg's alien-centric films. Feels like the scary-government parts of E.T., the chase action of Minority Report, and the big-hearted global span of Close Encounters. The building your house from childhood motif sits really well as a late-style take on ideas he has been working on his whole career, like Fabelmanns was to his career or The Irishman was for Scorsese's gangster films.
label: analysis

text: Lost Daughter really surprised me. Olivia Colman was incredible — one of the most interesting female characters I've seen. The entire end with the stolen doll and her desire to confess was remarkable. She is revealed as a kind of sociopathic woman capable of careless cruelty, but in a way that reflects onto the moral imperative of motherhood that all women face.
label: reaction

text: This was my introduction to Paul Mescal, who would be a big name within a couple years. It's not a huge role but he impressed me as special right off — not just a good-looking guy who can read lines. He had that way of making you wonder about a supporting character's story.
label: reaction

text: Lost Daughter made me think about my own mother and see her as more of a 3D person. Wondering if she ever felt that sort of exhaustion towards me, what being a mother meant to her, what her life would've been like without me.
label: reaction

text: No actor in world cinema has a stronger run of consecutive films than Al Pacino between 1971 and 1975 — The Panic in Needle Park, The Godfather, Scarecrow, Serpico, The Godfather Part II, and Dog Day Afternoon. These are six consecutive entries with no weak link. Several are widely considered among the greatest American films ever made.
label: hot_take

text: Every film John Cazale appeared in was nominated for Best Picture — both Godfathers, Dog Day Afternoon, The Conversation, The Deer Hunter. And his partner when he died was Meryl Streep, so even his girlfriend was a great actress.
label: hot_take

text: Godfather, Serpico, and Dog Day Afternoon — Sidney Lumet moved the audience back and forth like a teeter totter. In Serpico he keeps going up the chain of command and everyone tries to help him. Lumet's thesis: systems are corrupt, ignorance just leads to delay, no one person is at fault, the system is stronger than one person.
label: analysis

text: I'm rethinking how films should be evaluated depending on genre. A horror film might rely more on atmosphere and tension while a documentary depends on perspective and impact. Should some criteria matter more or less depending on the type of film? Can a film in a genre be great even if it fails at something usually considered important?
label: hot_take

text: Watch a movie and feel the way you feel about the experience first. Then try to understand what made you feel that way. Don't turn it into some qualification contest. By definition a box-checking system would only work for strict genre films — anything in between would be impossible to rate that way.
label: hot_take

text: Kristen Stewart said I just don't think it's possible to create radical vital work under capitalistic parameters. Clearly capitalism has been behind so much of what film even is. Have classic genres like 1930s Horror or 1940s Film Noir not been under capitalist parameters? Is there actually a closing down of artistic capacity in our era?
label: analysis

text: Artists have been making these same complaints since the beginning of time. That being said, it always needs to be said — you only get what you demand. Stewart is playing an old but necessary role in the arts.
label: hot_take

text: Stewart is describing a concept from Marxism called the superstructure. The superstructure rests on top of the mode of production and is inherently supportive of that same mode of production — it is one of the main ways that the mode of production is propagated.
label: analysis

text: Al Pacino left movies for 4 years when he lost the connection to acting. When he returned he wanted to get back to the kid who shouted Shakespearean monologues — that's what made him happy. Unfortunately younger moviegoers just don't like his style, and I wonder how long his legacy will last.
label: reaction

text: Pacino's 1971-1975 run is hands down my favorite decade run of any actor. Some of those movies are my favorites ever.
label: hot_take

text: I'm a genre abolitionist the same way I'm a gender abolitionist. Labels come with unnecessary baggage — they restrict freedom of expression by diluting what the film has to say into this big melting pot of what the genre has to say.
label: hot_take

text: Hollywood became so successful that it can no longer function. Blockbuster films that in the golden age would have been created in Hollywood now come from New Zealand, Hungary, Seoul. For the industry and the art form this is a good thing, but for the Hollywood system it's not.
label: analysis

text: How many passion projects by directors just handed money are actually properly good? Netflix threw money at loads of top directors and the resulting films showed they were turned down for a reason. Kelly Reichardt's The Mastermind, Die My Love, High Life — these aren't films ruined by compromising, they're highly regarded directors incapable of getting the most out of what they're chasing.
label: hot_take

text: Over a decades-long career Robert Altman directed dozens of movies. Outside of Nashville, McCabe and Mrs. Miller, The Player, Gosford Park — what hidden gem deserves more attention? My answer is Vincent and Theo (1990), a sad beautiful moving story about the love-hate relationship between Vincent and Theo van Gogh that I've never heard anyone talk about.
label: reaction

text: California Split is my personal favorite Altman film, made during arguably his most successful period between The Long Goodbye and Nashville. The ending is so subtle about making its point that people just didn't appreciate the ambiguity. It's basically a comedy until it isn't. Elliot Gould and George Segal were never better.
label: hot_take

text: I never hear people talk about The Company but I found it really lovely — almost trance-inducing in its resistance to plot. It reminds me most of documentary filmmaker Frederick Wiseman. The viewer is invited to simply spend time in a very specific space with the people who occupy it, with very little judgment of them.
label: analysis

text: Altman in general is underrated. He's the greatest American director and rarely gets mentioned in those lists. California Split might be my favorite movie of all time — endlessly rewatchable, incredibly layered and intricate from a production and creativity perspective.
label: hot_take

text: Every successful director has that one movie that defines them. Trainspotting is Danny Boyle's. What I found most fascinating is its non-judgmental portrayal of heroin addiction. The lack of portrayal of normal people living alongside our characters feels extremely intentional — those normal people are not relevant. We stay with these characters and only them.
label: analysis

text: What makes Trainspotting endure is that it never turns its characters into symbols. They're selfish, funny, awful, vulnerable — sometimes all in the same scene. The style is flashy but the empathy underneath is what sticks.
label: analysis

text: Trainspotting endures because of the attention to relationships and especially the complexity of male friendship. The film's theme revolves around the line But he's a mate so what can you do? And ultimately we see what you can do at the end.
label: analysis

text: T2 Trainspotting came and went with not much fanfare and seems to have been forgotten. But it's a great meditation on friendships and getting older. The original is a classic that helped define an era — there's probably an entire generation of kids that had its poster on their college dorm room walls.
label: reaction

text: Vertigo has completely unraveled me. It's been a long time since a film struck such a deep emotional chord. I have reservations about the far-fetched twist — I believe the film would have been more powerful had it remained in the realm of a surreal dream and ended as a pure psychological mindfuck.
label: reaction

text: Both you and Scottie suffer from the same problem — you fall in love with the fictional idea of a woman rather than the actual woman herself. When you meet a woman who meets your standards, instead of relating to her as a fellow human being you put her on a pedestal and obsess about her as a trophy or art object.
label: analysis

text: There are two lungs to the problem with Scottie, and it's arguably only one of them that fits the reading of look what men do to women. The empathic reading of Scottie is a non-gendered one — limerence and romantic love fed by a sense of mystery and sudden loss. Ghosting can be haunting because it is unresolved, as can any sudden loss.
label: analysis

text: Sam Peckinpah's Straw Dogs has one of the best works of editing and montage I've ever seen. The final siege on the house blew my mind — close-ups, detail shots, dutch angles, rapid editing at just the right moments to ramp up the tension, slow-motion camera. All while Hoffman's physical condition gradually deteriorates until you find him on those wooden steps with a drenched face and dark circles.
label: analysis

text: Sergio Leone had an amazing sense of rhythm. The final three-way showdown in The Good The Bad and The Ugly is a masterpiece of editing and montage — it turns basically three guys standing still staring at each other into pure cinema for five minutes.
label: hot_take

text: Hitchcock's Psycho shower scene holds a strange fascination and power to this day. Some of the cuts don't make literal sense — there are jump cuts and blatant continuity flaws. But it all works together to create this amazing sequence which continues after the murder to that eye close-up on the floor and the drain.
label: analysis

text: Fosse's editing works best in Cabaret — the angles he chooses, the fast editing, the strange details he draws the eye to. He's a master of theatrical choreography AND a master of choreography on film. His film All That Jazz has similar flashes of brilliance, but his love of montage boils over into something that at times can be a lot of work.
label: analysis

text: JFK is my pick for great editing. You really feel the experience of a cocaine addict telling you the Kennedy assassination didn't go down that way for 3 hours. The pacing of the cuts is absolutely manic and relentless. The montage throughout Costner's testimony at the end is the easy high point, but the whole thing is cut like a roller coaster.
label: analysis

text: Do film schools really teach that there is no such thing as misinterpretation? I read someone make this claim recently. Seems counterintuitive for film schools to teach that and also verifiably false — especially given all the talk of media literacy lately.
label: hot_take

text: If you have an interpretation that is well observed, argued, and rooted in the actual contents of the film, you've done the work correctly and it's hard to say it's wrong. I remember one class where I got a mediocre grade on a paper about Boyz in the Hood because my analysis was obvious and shallow — pointing out that Singleton is saying gang violence is bad by cutting from a stats title card to a stop sign isn't deep thinking.
label: analysis

text: I had extraordinarily high expectations walking into Obsession expecting a masterpiece after its 95% RT score. The story uses at least three common tropes — Be Careful What You Wish For, love potion, and the obsessed lover. Perhaps Parasite has spoiled me but I did not expect this to be the actual story.
label: reaction

text: You shouldn't apply criticism to something from what you hoped or expected it to be — you can only judge it for what it is and whether it does that successfully. Otherwise you're playing an impossible game of wish fulfillment. If this wasn't such a hyped movie would you feel as disappointed or would you think it was a slick little film that was interesting enough?
label: analysis

text: The ambiguity makes Obsession interesting. The inversion of victim and monster between Nikki and Bear is pretty great — Bear being the perpetrator but also the victim, such a creep of a guy and yet not entirely unsympathetic. Not something you see very often.
label: analysis

text: Everyone keeps hyping Poor Things as the ultimate story about female emancipation but Bella didn't find freedom — she just climbed the class ladder. She starts as literal property, becomes a sex worker, has an emotional breakdown about inequality on the cruise ship, then goes right back and inherits Godwin's massive estate. She didn't break any system. She just used old money to buy her way into the 1%.
label: analysis

text: Everything is pretty cynical if you hold it to a revolutionary standard. To move from being property to holding a certain degree of self-actualization is definitely a victory for the individual character. Unfortunately it's not a victory for the proletariat but not many stories are.
label: hot_take

text: Lanthimos combines the worst qualities of Lars von Trier and Wes Anderson while lacking the genuine inventiveness of either. He wants his films to be perceived as strange and controversial yet they always feel safe-weird, simultaneously trying not to alienate anyone. There is nothing surreal or thought-provoking beyond surface-level zaniness and violence.
label: hot_take

text: My biggest issue with the ending is that Bella's final act in a movie all about discovering her own agency is to violently strip that agency away from someone else — and the film does not even try to interrogate the ramifications of that. It treats it like a fairy tale happy ending despite undermining the whole point of Bella's journey.
label: analysis

text: Poor Things is what men think female liberation looks like. There is too much emphasis on sex as a vehicle for liberation. My problem was less with how the film handles class and more about the male gaze throughout.
label: hot_take

text: Babylon was one of the best movies I've seen in the theater. Right from the beginning everyone was hooked. We WERE at the party. During the Kinescope day sequence the whole theater was thrown into the desert set. The ending montage — the music, the sounds — was a unique moment. Damien Chazelle KNOWS why people go to the theater.
label: reaction

text: When I saw Dune in IMAX there was a guy behind me who was mildly high. His occasional audible reactions made my experience better — whooooa, oh my god, no way. Just barely whispered. If you watch the sandworm attack and calmly whisper that's not good while high, you are the steadying presence I need in my life.
label: reaction

text: One of my favorite movie experiences was seeing Magic Mike in a crowded theater in Chicago. Half the crowd was drunk middle-aged ladies just having an absolute blast heckling the screen. At one point someone screamed HE GOT A SAFE and I don't know if I have ever laughed harder in a movie theater.
label: reaction

text: I saw Hereditary in a full cinema and there is a scene about halfway through that completely changes the atmosphere. The shocked gasp from every single person in the theater let me know that nobody else was expecting it either.
label: reaction

text: I loved watching Get Out in a movie theater full of vocal black folks. The collective groan when the police car first rolls up at the end — and the shift in emotions as expectations are subverted — is one of my most memorable movie experiences. It felt like the audience reaction was part of the movie itself.
label: reaction

text: Little Women was one of the best theater experiences I've ever had. When Beth died you could hear everyone in that room crying. I walked out feeling like it was opening night of an Avengers movie with how engaged the audience was. Nothing beats the big screen when it's a good experience.
label: reaction

text: A rowdy theater for the right movie is a joy. Horror movies and comedies are great in a theater. But in practice all the notable memories I have of other people's audible reactions are universally negative — there are just so many ways other people can detract from the experience and so few in which they can enhance it.
label: hot_take

text: I saw Paranormal Activity in the theater in middle school — probably the perfect time and place for it. Nobody was taking it seriously yet were still yelling from the jump scares. One part shows a character with a wound and an audience member yelled SHE GOT BIT then when the character said it looks like something bit you the same person yelled I TOLD YOU.
label: reaction

text: I just finished viewing Phantom Thread and my wife and I almost walked out due to the audience reacting as if they were watching a rom-com — actual knee-slapping laughter during scenes that were tense with an undertone of frightening humor. Most inappropriate audience laughter stems from people being uncomfortable or challenged by a film.
label: reaction

text: Phantom Thread is funny — intentionally so. I would say its genre is a dark romantic comedy. It is funny like Eyes Wide Shut is funny.
label: hot_take

text: I know exactly what you're talking about with inappropriate laughter — the most vivid memory I have is a similar phenomenon completely ruining my experience of seeing Moonlight. Every little tension-breaking aside was met with loud disruptive laughter. It can really ruin a viewing experience of a deep dramatic movie.
label: reaction

text: With the recent success of Backrooms, which recalls films like Koyaanisqatsi in unflinchingly looking at consumerism and liminal spaces, the general moviegoing audience is starting to become more interested in films that immerse themselves in what current life truly feels like. I hear the claim a lot that Southland Tales would be a huge hit nowadays for its hyperreal presentation of culture slamming against itself.
label: analysis

text: Parasite exposed hidden truths about class in society and self worth — the tragedy of struggling just to get by, the loss of dignity one may feel having a superior reprimand you for not being enthusiastic enough about your work. I had a visceral reaction to that movie and I think the same was true for a lot of people in the United States, which is ironic being a Korean film.
label: reaction

text: Movies about AI and social media feel contrived and tired already. It's such low hanging fruit and doesn't really deal with the true implications — just uses it as a gimmick for some B-horror movie. Movies with messages about the dangers of AI and capitalism have already been made 50 years ago like Terminator and Blade Runner. We just didn't listen.
label: hot_take

text: Any piece of artwork is a reflection of the time in which it's produced as much as the time in which it's set. The emotional core of any piece won't be about current events but about the aftermath of current events — things yet to unfold. Consider how the western as a genre has shifted over time as cultural attitudes towards American exceptionalism and manifest destiny have changed.
label: analysis

text: I watched Mulholland Drive last night and it genuinely blew my mind. Magnificent. Masterpiece. As someone who enjoys dark atmospheric psychological thrillers I was literally speechless afterwards and ran right to Reddit and ChatGPT for insight and cross-referencing symbolism. I understood it as: the first 80% of the movie is the dream and the last 20% is reality after it ends.
label: reaction

text: Mulholland Drive is one of the greatest Los Angeles films, along with the obvious picks by Wilder, Polanski, Altman, PT Anderson, Tarantino, and LA Confidential. Lynch really loved LA and you get the sense he had a good understanding of both the allure it has for hopefuls getting off buses and planes, and the shabbier more mercenary side they often encounter.
label: analysis

text: I hate the sentiment that you're not supposed to know what a Lynch film is about. What am I supposed to think then? Is a Rothko painting just some colors and squares? Obviously there's a point even if you're wrong — otherwise anyone could do anything and record it and get the same result.
label: hot_take

text: What strikes me as odd is that The Hunt is hilariously exaggerated in what liberal-leaning people think and do. Meanwhile Civil War shows the opposite side of the spectrum except it's almost so realistic it doesn't seem exaggerated. Watching these two movies back to back honestly got me thinking about how each side is portrayed.
label: reaction

text: The Hunt is a riff on The Most Dangerous Game, first published in 1924 and adapted hundreds of times. While the original was about the irony of a big game hunter falling prey to a richer hunter, class disparities have been a central part of its retellings since the start.
label: analysis

text: The Hunt is lousy and artless — plays like a conservative fever dream. Both films are propaganda-adjacent with not-so-subtle political intentions to stir up fear in a particular voting base and keep people fighting about red vs blue instead of rich vs poor. That's the main intent of the films, not to produce a work of art.
label: hot_take

text: Civil War was incredible in its realism and very clearly a criticism of the right, though it tries to show the left realistically too — not as heroes. The movie didn't seem unrealistic about what each side would be capable of once the first bullet is fired. Each side demonizes and dehumanizes the other too much, and if it continues it can only lead to events like in Civil War.
label: analysis

text: Armando searches for proof of his dead mother but never finds it; Fernando finds proof of his dead father. The movie connects the act of resistance to the act of remembering. The underground doesn't get Armando to safety, but they create a record of his story that passes years later to the archivist who steals the material for Fernando. It's all the same story.
label: analysis

text: The film is saying remembering is required even after a successful revolution. Brazil elected a fascist member of the military dictatorship after a prosperous era under Lula. Filho is trying to get across the those who don't remember history idea without being cliche.
label: analysis

text: The story Fernando tells about his nightmares stopping after seeing Jaws solidified it for me. He could only move on by confronting what scared him rather than let it live on in his imagination.
label: analysis

text: The abrupt switch — cutting the potential final chase and final moments from the main character with no catharsis, no clear answers, no payback — leaves the audience helpless and confused. We don't say goodbye to Armando. Kind of the similar way families who are victims of the dictatorship feel. They also don't say their goodbyes — they just read the fate of their loved ones in the papers.
label: analysis

text: I was like wait, what, it's all just a flashback? I was having a wonderful time watching a very well-recreated time period and then — wait, is that an iPhone? Did they mess up? I felt like the rug was pulled out from under me. I just chalked it up to being trained by a lifetime of North American movies to expect certain things.
label: reaction

text: Modern cinema irritates me deeply. The surplus of digital aesthetics has produced a landscape of films that have more in common with video than film. The loss of textures and practicality has produced quickly produced content. A bad film in the past still involved skill and effort — decisions had to be made on set, everything was heavier and more expensive. Effects were done in camera.
label: hot_take

text: It's amazing how much more I enjoy even bad films from the analog film age. I will sit through a crappy movie from the 90s or early 2000s simply because they are so rich with warmth, color, and texture. It's pleasing to look at and I don't mind what's happening with the story as much.
label: hot_take

text: The first Jurassic Park still delivers amazing moments. Although the CGI is crude by today's standards, the dinosaurs viewed under bright uncorrected natural light appear almost tangible. In the recent series, even though new dinosaurs keep appearing, they simply look fake — the more CGI a movie uses, the darker and more overly blue the screen becomes.
label: analysis

text: We have entered an interesting phase of horror with semi-elevated films. The first wave like Babadook, Get Out, and Hereditary were very focused on elevated metaphorical aspects. A second wave like Obsession, Weapons, and Smile 2 still have intellectual substance but are first and foremost well-crafted genre movies. Judging Obsession as if it intended to be the definitive piece on patriarchal violence seems unfair.
label: analysis

text: The movie ends just like MacReady's chess game at the beginning — he beats the computer by destroying it, and beats the alien by destroying the base. In both cases he won but at the cost of his own defeat. He's also indirectly responsible for five of the ten deaths. Clearly his leadership skills were not the greatest.
label: analysis

text: Not knowing is the point and it's what makes The Thing's ending so great. The characters don't know, so the audience doesn't know. Carpenter has said he has a definitive answer but won't tell — I'm convinced he left it open ended on purpose and I kind of love that.
label: hot_take

text: If one of them was the Thing at the end, it could easily kill the other — there's no one else left and no reason to keep up the ruse. It makes no sense that either of them is the Thing and doesn't just kill the other right then and there. It's a great bleak ending but the logic of either being infected doesn't hold up.
label: analysis

text: One interpretation of the last scene of The Searchers is that the men who protect us can't live among us. The harsh reality of the world requires ruthless violent men who understand what civilized society cannot. They willingly sacrifice themselves to exile in order to protect us. I can see that interpretation — it jives with Ford's politics of the time.
label: analysis

text: I saw The Searchers when it was first released as a child. Spending all those years searching for the kidnapped girl is a movie of its times, and it is still true today that ties of blood family go very deep. The last poignant scene in the doorway expresses the lone life of the cowboy.
label: reaction

text: I was so let down by Marty Supreme. My biggest complaint was how little I felt like I could connect to anyone in this story. I loved Good Time and Uncut Gems — there was unbearable tension and love/hate characters throughout. But there was just so little to like about Marty Supreme. It was so one dimensional.
label: reaction

text: Structurally Marty Supreme and Uncut Gems are very similar but the endings are inverted. I saw the ending as Marty trying desperately to lead a life of fame and failing, with his descent into mundane fatherhood representing in his mind the ultimate failure. His recognition of himself in the child meant he knew there was no way out.
label: analysis

text: Marty Supreme would have been much more impressive if Uncut Gems didn't already exist. Sandler adds humanity to his role that Chalamet can't match. Not only is Uncut Gems more tightly woven and better paced, but Sandler adds so much to the project that can't be overstated.
label: hot_take

text: Marty Supreme is just as layered as Uncut Gems with a similarly complicated anti-hero. Its themes of ambition, survival, Jewish identity, post-Holocaust Jewish identity, and capitalism all relate to the characters. Ultimately the movie is about the lengths someone will go to for survival — how much humiliation they're willing to go through and how much of themselves they're willing to lose.
label: analysis

text: The Tree of Life is the greatest film I've ever seen but I couldn't tell you why — it's almost like all the words escape me. After rewatching with the director's cut I can convincingly say this is the most gorgeous thought-provoking and transcendental cinema that has ever graced the screen. It doesn't fall into any criteria when it comes to rating or reviewing — it's something you can only experience not decipher.
label: reaction

text: Tree of Life is in my all-time top 5. I've always viewed the creation scene as a preface saying: we are going to discuss heavy topics, but remember that you and your problems don't matter in the grand scheme of things. It's one of those rare watches where you just feel rather than disseminate.
label: reaction

text: The spirituality of Tree of Life felt very shallow — borderline I'm 14 and this is deep material. The kind of rumination that would be fun shared between bong rips at 1am at a college party, but comes across as self-indulgent when put to film.
label: hot_take

text: Tree of Life is the greatest American film ever made and deserves more respect. As does A Hidden Life — you can't tell me that's not a masterpiece either. And incredibly timely, as if there's a time that wouldn't be the case.
label: hot_take

text: The original backrooms image went viral precisely because of what it implied rather than what it showed — infinite empty space. Liminality is the state of being in-between, a transitional threshold like an empty terminal at 3am, a place built to be passed through not occupied. The Backrooms film is unfortunately extremely bogged down and betrays this concept.
label: analysis

text: The Backrooms film didn't betray its concept — it just went with a concept that didn't match what some viewers wanted. It's ultimately a zombie/haunted house movie with a somewhat liminal aesthetic, and it's fairly solid on those straightforward terms. The director has said it was never the goal to emulate the YouTube series.
label: hot_take

text: The real horror of the Backrooms is being trapped in an endless featureless extradimensional labyrinth with no means of escape. The movie doesn't communicate any real passage of time — Clark has mapped out the place after only being there once and resorts to eating zombie flesh after what feels like a day. Poking around the Backrooms isn't scary on its own, it's the thought of spending days weeks months there.
label: analysis

text: I'm watching War and Peace (1966) and I'm mesmerized by the sheer scale and spectacle — the battle sequences and ballroom one-shots literally drop my jaw. But I feel like the film doesn't try that much to connect us with the characters. Certain things happen too fast — Andrei's wife dies and I don't really care because the film didn't spend enough time with her.
label: reaction

text: Keep in mind that the target audience — Russians — would have already been aware of all the characters and their motivations. War and Peace was and is part of the school curriculum in Russia, like Shakespeare or Dickens in the UK. Pretty much everyone would have read the book.
label: analysis

text: I've seen most of peak Cronenberg — Scanners, Videodrome, The Fly, Naked Lunch, Dead Ringers — and he is easily among my favorite directors. I then watched A History of Violence and found it somewhat mediocre. I then jumped to Crimes of the Future and The Shrouds and really enjoyed them. His mid-career films like Spider, Eastern Promises, Cosmopolis haven't been discussed as much.
label: reaction

text: A History of Violence is Cronenberg's greatest achievement as a director — he makes a classic Hollywood film that is still 100% Cronenberg. A Dangerous Method was embarrassing to watch. Maps to the Stars was bad. Cosmopolis was boring. Dead Ringers is great. Such an interesting director and auteur.
label: hot_take

text: A History of Violence is a perfectly fine and entertaining movie but it's not really saying anything original and it definitely isn't remotely as weirdly creative as his best movies. Videodrome, The Fly, and Crash are my personal favorites. Knowing it's a Cronenberg film actually takes something away from it — it just isn't why I watch a Cronenberg film.
label: hot_take

text: Eastern Promises is extremely well-done with tremendous acting from Viggo Mortensen. It's one of the few higher-quality Russian gangster films easily accessible to western audiences. But it's not even close to the psychological depth of Dead Ringers or Crash.
label: hot_take

text: Naked Lunch might be the second-best Cronenberg film. You might need to know about the source material and Burroughs and the movement he was a part of, but it is definitely not unfocused.
label: hot_take

text: There is a distinction between Cronenberg's adaptations of others' work and films that come directly from him. He is a master adaptor. Like many great artists he returned to more personal work later in his career and those films might be his most psychologically interesting.
label: analysis

text: I don't see Backrooms as fundamentally a horror movie — more of a sci-fi mystery. None of Parsons' work goes for intense scares so I don't find the lack of that to be any issue. Backrooms was flawed but extremely unique and interesting. Obsession was great execution of scenes and trends we've seen for a decade. I don't find them comparable whatsoever but I loved both.
label: hot_take

text: This movie changed my direction in life. I still have my ticket stub from the original theatrical run — it included a section saying I saw The Tree of Life and it changed how I felt about blank.
label: reaction

text: The Dead Zone might be Cronenberg's most grounded film up to that point in his career. As much as I was swept up in the plot I was equally taken along by the emotions of the main character — his confusion, bitterness, resentment. Christopher Walken seems to bring emotional seriousness just by default — even when his character is grinning you can feel the pain underneath.
label: reaction

text: The Dead Zone is a masterpiece. They somehow took a huge unwieldy novel and distilled it down to the essence of John Smith's story, all grounded by an incredible heartrending performance by Walken. He runs the emotional gamut from humble happy teacher to bitter survivor to emotional recluse to someone who accepts his fate as a reluctant hero.
label: hot_take

text: The Dead Zone has one of the most bittersweet and heartwarming endings of all time. He did what he needed to do with Stillson, but the emotionally correct ending was that he needed to let go of his girl and say goodbye. A rare movie that understands what feels right emotionally is what the viewer needs to feel satisfied.
label: reaction

text: What stood out most about Oppenheimer was how determined Nolan was to avoid condensing real figures into fictional composite characters. Instead of the friend and the coworker we get a huge ensemble. The downside is this decision deprived the film of a clear dramatic core — I felt an uncomfortable void in the experience and left restive and unabsorbed.
label: analysis

text: Nolan is probably more of a glorified Michael Bay — they have a lot in common. He doesn't understand the serious subjects he tackles. It's big thunderous spectacle and pure entertainment without much substance. That's perfectly fine, but let's be honest about what it is.
label: hot_take

text: The bigger issue was making Oppenheimer vs Strauss too much a good guys vs bad guys with an artificially stand-up-and-cheer Final Takedown. The film needed to give Oppenheimer a win at the end, which undercut his more emotionally authentic downfall.
label: analysis

text: The first James Bond movie came out in 1962 and Bond has been relevant for over 60 years. One advantage Bond currently enjoys over Marvel and DC is that it occasionally went away for a while. If there was a new Bond movie every year people would get sick of him. The very strength of the franchise — its ability to change — is also what stirs up the most controversy.
label: analysis

text: I love Bond but I'm very skeptical about the future. The Broccolis have sold out to Amazon and Jeff Bezos will likely water it down, dilute it with spinoff series, and optimize it for streaming. I hope I'm wrong but I have zero trust in Amazon to properly steward it.
label: hot_take

text: I'd really like to see Bond go back to being a series of semi-related adventures with a few recurring characters — less focused on lore and continuity and more on variety. Ironically Knives Out is a great template for how to do that, with enough flexibility to have one that's a family drama, one that's a tech satire, and one that's a gothic thriller.
label: hot_take

text: Weapons will be remembered past the 2020s — memorable editing style, memorable opening scene, memorable cast, memorable villain. Horror is definitely at its peak currently. Sinners and the Terrifier films would be my second and third picks. Art the Clown has cemented himself into the Horror Hall of Fame.
label: hot_take

text: Aftersun is a real step forward in creating vulnerable personal cinema in a stylish way. Tár is amazing in every way — a rare example of a movie that carefully studies aspects of modern culture and analyzes them smartly while remaining fun and engaging. La Chimera is an artistic masterpiece that boldly innovates on film's artistic presentation.
label: hot_take

text: The Substance is going to become a teenage sleepover classic — just the right levels of gory and sexy that'll still work in decades time, like An American Werewolf in London, 80s slasher movies, or Jennifer's Body.
label: hot_take

text: I often feel disconnected from characters in Nolan films, to a lesser degree with Kubrick, and an even greater degree with Snyder. Their characters don't display much humanity or weakness — they feel robotic in an unnaturally perfect and effective way. Their goals are so lofty that when they succeed it feels unearned.
label: hot_take

text: The secret to people connecting with a character is to give that character a lot of close-ups and not give other characters much if any. You might think I am kidding. I am not. Try it, you'll see.
label: hot_take

text: Antonioni's L'Avventura deliberately avoids identification with characters by refusing reaction shots in dialogue — characters often speak with their backs to us, and sometimes both characters have their backs turned entirely. Long takes in impressive landscapes overwhelm the characters. They don't act very rationally but we don't know enough about their pasts to understand why.
label: analysis

text: I don't care if I have nothing in common with a character or if they're unlikable. They need to feel convincing as entities — consistent, operating under certain logic and principles — rather than serving as an illustration of some message or a tool to deliver it.
label: analysis

text: Pierce Brosnan was the best Bond in terms of the classic idea of who James Bond is. He just had a bad run — Goldeneye being the only really good movie. TND and TWINE are fine but not spectacular and Die Another Day is potentially the worst Bond movie.
label: hot_take

text: Casino Royale is one of the most essential entries in the Bond series. It raised the prestige of the franchise after damage done by silly Brosnan movies at the end. Skyfall also took the character seriously and offered commentary on the character itself.
label: hot_take

text: I never got the Weapons overhype. It's an extremely fun horror film but there's nothing groundbreaking that would make it stand out from other good films in the genre. Ditto for the Terrifier films. Sinners might get remembered because of the insane critical reception and Oscar nominations.
label: hot_take

text: The Dune trilogy, unless the third completely bungles it, is adapting an ever-relevant story with crisp visuals that won't age badly unlike a lot of CGI eyesores today. It'll end up like the LOTR trilogy where it'll always have hardcore fans and will be pulled out whenever anyone wants an epic movie night marathon.
label: hot_take

text: Oppenheimer whilst being a biopic really isn't — it's a portrait of the men who created the nuclear bomb and how that got turned into the atomic bomb project. All done by highly intelligent but off and possibly sociopathic people who took huge risks they didn't know would kill us all, and how that mentality continued through to the Cold War and MAD doctrine.
label: analysis

text: I get a total disconnect when a character does horrible things to others and I'm expected to empathize with them because they're flawed and whiny. There's some victim/aggressor reversal going on that I just can't handle — examples include Zero Dark Thirty and its torture sequences.
label: reaction

text: Why do people think they always have to like the protagonist? Sometimes the entire point of the movie is that this is not a likable person. Oppenheimer was kind of an arrogant dick who invented the worst weapon in the world. Humbert was a delusional pedophile. Jack Torrance was a drunken domestic abuser. Normalize hating people in movies.
label: hot_take

text: I read War and Peace last year and saw the film shortly after. Those characters and incidents were portrayed proportionately with the book — I don't think Tolstoy draws those characters especially deeply either. The whole incident where Natasha is ruined literally takes place over the space of a week in the novel — Tolstoy's emphasis is that she's a bored impetuous teen.
label: analysis

text: What struck me most watching The Dead Zone was the prescience of the narrative. Disregarding the fantastical elements, I can't help but see the movie as a mirror reflection of the USA's current state of political affairs.
label: reaction

text: The first time I saw Paris Texas the film pulls you right in depriving you of any context. Yet you feel emotional and sad. I've always felt the same about Shine On You Crazy Diamond by Pink Floyd — we don't hear the first words until halfway through the song. Michael Mann's Miami Vice really felt like a modern orchestration of electronic and pop pieces.
label: reaction

text: Dead Man features one of the most immersive sound-related cinematic experiences of modern motion pictures. Neil Young's subtle dronings enhance the timeless landscape of the movie. From the get go the all-encompassing music feels like some mesmerizing sonic mantra. You're almost watching sounds.
label: analysis

text: The fractured surreal qualities of Has' The Hourglass Sanatorium are reminiscent of Mahler's Seventh Symphony. The dreamlike shifts in the film — ideas being introduced and then vanishing at the drop of a hat — feel like the cinematic equivalent of that modal mixture where major and minor modes exchange so extensively you never know where you are.
label: analysis

text: Tarantino called biopics just big excuses for actors to win Oscars — corrupted cinema. Fame-based biopics I generally agree with — there isn't anything inherently interesting about famous actors or singers. They've become formulaic bores: the struggle, the success, the excess, the comeback. Ultimately they're just vehicles for actors to do impressions that impress award voters.
label: hot_take

text: I'm fatigued with biopics as much as anyone. But something like Goodfellas is absolutely a biopic — many of Scorsese's films are. The difference is that Henry Hill is not famous and you don't have to know who he is to enjoy the movie. That's the key distinction between good and bad biopics.
label: analysis

text: It's reductive to say all biopics are Oscar bait. In 1962 Lawrence of Arabia came out — ostensibly a biopic and one of the greatest films of all time. Every genre has a gimmick that can get overplayed and award bait tends to be the gimmick of biopics, but some of the greatest films ever made have been biopics.
label: hot_take

text: Biopics tend to struggle because true stories are not always logical or structurally sound, or even inherently interesting. You need a struggle in a story, some sort of challenge the hero has to overcome. Real lives don't always provide that natural dramatic structure.
label: analysis

text: Joaquin Phoenix in The Master — most actors playing Freddie Quell would have led with the trauma and alcoholism. Phoenix leads with the physical deformation of that trauma. The way his hands hang, the way his torso curves like he's been carrying something for years. There's a scene where he's just standing on a beach and he looks like a question mark made of bones. The performance lives in his posture before he says a word.
label: analysis

text: Tom Hardy plays Mad Max like he forgets how to talk between each of his lines and has to reteach himself every time he has something to say. A replacement actor might do something similar for ten minutes but would want Max to come back to himself over the course of the movie. The way Hardy plays it, the whole episode is passing for Max like a dream.
label: analysis

text: Rutger Hauer as Roy Batty in Blade Runner — his improvised adjustments to his final monologue, cutting out a bunch of memories and getting right to the crux with all those moments will be lost in time like tears in rain, adds so much to the whole replicant discourse. Absolutely brilliant.
label: analysis

text: Christian Bale in American Psycho commits to Patrick Bateman as a hollow performative creature who's constantly acting within the film itself. His phone confession at the end conveys terror at being caught, the sick pleasure he gets from killing, alternately resigned and manic despair, and ultimately acceptance. It's horrifying, tragic, funny and absolutely riveting.
label: analysis

text: Florence Pugh in Midsommar uses facial expression to convey so much and never seems over the top the way a lot of actresses do in horror films. Paul Dano's scream in Little Miss Sunshine — other actors might have gone with quieter rage but it felt really raw. Christoph Waltz saying That's a bingo with a giant smile in Inglourious Basterds.
label: analysis

text: With the fall of Marvel there hasn't been until the last few years viral monoculture films that everyone will see regardless of interest level. Now the rise of YouTube filmmakers is bringing intense online fandom to the cinema. Films like Sinners, Talk to Me, Obsession, and Backrooms all have this intense virality — a let's all embrace this film no matter what quality to them.
label: analysis

text: Modern virality is a different beast from traditional blockbuster hype. It's like a meme that hits so perfectly and aptly describes the current climate that everyone shares it and it becomes a piece of monoculture. It's weird how defensive people get if you say you didn't care for the viral movie du jour — reacting as if it's their own film.
label: hot_take

text: The Apprentice is like some kind of cursed painting you can't look away from even though looking at it takes a toll on your sanity. There's this weird claustrophobia from its up close handheld cinematography and its two central figures. Trump and Cohn are not people you can ever enjoy being with and this movie doesn't give us the relief of watching immoral people through the eyes of a moral protagonist.
label: analysis

text: The movie worked mostly because of that absolutely ghoulish Jeremy Strong performance. Movie Roy Cohn is a soulless man who doesn't feel much besides anger when things don't go his way and gratification when they do. The score and glitzy production added an effective artificial layer of sheen that captured the superficiality of the man.
label: analysis

text: Manchester by the Sea was probably the only movie where grief is so realistically handled. The flashbacks feel like Lee's literal thoughts — that's why they feel sudden and jarring. The screenplay is so mundane with totally everyday conversations, nothing over the top. To convey grief in such everyday dialogue you actually have to face it.
label: reaction

text: Watching Manchester by the Sea you can tell Lonergan has actually been around actual people. So many movies have dialogue written by someone who hasn't been around actual people in forever. The subtle humor didn't feel forced or separated from the bigger story — it helped show how the relationship grew between Affleck and the boy.
label: analysis

text: I was unaware of how much of a self-loather and failure I felt I was when this movie came out, so I was weirdly captivated and comforted watching Manchester by the Sea. The police station scene and the fridge door not closing — I felt seen. I've learned to be more self-aware as I got older but I still carry that critical part of me.
label: reaction

text: Casey Affleck's character was subtly better off mentally at the end than at the start. When he's fixing the water heater near the end he's a little more alive and engaged. The ball-bouncing scene makes clear he and Patrick are going to stay connected — it's a happy-ish ending where by bonding with his nephew over shared grief he eases his own burden.
label: analysis

text: I just finished reading Shane Carruth's screenplay for his unmade film A Topiary. It's a very long and challenging screenplay composed mostly in expositional shorthand — Carruth doesn't seem interested in spelling out extraneous details for outsiders as he clearly has all the concepts expanded in his head. Still I think its intent can be understood.
label: reaction

text: Upstream Color was a drastic change from Primer, not just in production quality but in the lack of dialogue. I felt very dissatisfied when I finished it. It showed Carruth knows how to be diverse — it was much more atmospheric and emotional than Primer, which was more story driven.
label: reaction

text: Upstream Color is an allegory of consumer society. The two protagonists have been hosts to a parasite that sucks their will, destroys their confidence, indebts them, and forces them into low status jobs they have to work to survive. The parasite is capitalism made literal.
label: analysis

text: I didn't find A Topiary quite so esoteric. The subject matter is inherently weird but Carruth lays out each new concept in an understandable way. As long as you take a moment with each new term he introduces and really make sure you've got it pictured before moving on you'll be okay — otherwise it's like missing a week of algebra class where you're screwed when the next concept builds on the last.
label: reaction

text: I was actually disappointed at how non-mysterious A Topiary was in the end — the replicators gonna replicate trope is very old. The bit where blueprints for making the Choruses are encoded in random phenomena is an interesting twist but it never gets developed at all.
label: reaction

text: I just finished a 2 month Alfred Hitchcock film festival watching 4 films a week. My main takeaway: Hitchcock essentially only ever made 2 horror movies — Psycho and The Birds. In reality most of his movies are thrillers filled with comedy. His most common narrative device, the wrong man, relies on cops mistaking the protagonist for the killer and hunting him down based on terrible assumptions.
label: reaction

text: Hitchcock wasn't ACAB in a political sense — he just had a phobia of police stemming from a childhood incident where his father attempted a scare-him-straight prank. His films were just ways of dealing with these anxieties. In classic Hollywood you literally couldn't criticize police actions unless you made it clear it was a lone wolf who doesn't represent the department.
label: analysis

text: There were ten years and then three decades of reruns of Alfred Hitchcock Presents, which while chock full of comedy during his segments was basically a thriller show. Because of the show's popularity and long run this is likely in the forefront of more people's minds than his films — which explains why people associate him primarily with horror and thrillers rather than comedy.
label: analysis

text: Nolan has a very distinct style he has been refining his entire career — a strong love for practical effects, a visual sensibility missing from most high budget films, and his three-scene-intercutting which is littered throughout every single one of his films. It isn't to everyone's taste but it is uniquely his.
label: analysis

text: Spielberg went from being the best showman in the business making top-notch blockbusters to making humanist message movies in 1993 with the one-two punch of Jurassic Park and Schindler's List. Those two movies feel like total opposites and a sort of passing of the torch from one half of his career to the other. His career has two very distinct halves.
label: analysis

text: Fellini started out with the Neorealists, making very grounded sedate films. Then he started moving the camera a little more in each film until by 8½ he was fully Felliniesque. He feels like the standard bearer for the abrupt stylistic shift that divides a career into two distinct halves.
label: analysis

text: Kubrick fits the two-halves idea — pre and post 2001: A Space Odyssey. All of his stuff after 1968 feels different. There's a certain pace unique to him that you only see in those later movies — the way he cuts between singles of people talking, his use of one-point perspective.
label: analysis

text: Cuaron had a huge stylistic shift with Y Tu Mama Tambien. Everything leading up to that had been decent but workmanlike. Y Tu saw Cuaron shift to a long-take oriented approach to mise en scene, very handheld camera work, and more naturalistic performances. He went from a fairly run-of-the-mill director to an auteur.
label: analysis

text: I was recommended Koyaanisqatsi after watching Candyman because they were both scored by Philip Glass. First few minutes I was about to write it off as another experimental avant-garde piece. Then I felt drawn in and it began to hypnotize me. Then the hard shift into industrial humanity took me back. Finished it and decided it was ultimately decent and went to bed.
label: reaction

text: Koyaanisqatsi manages to make you feel like a spectator to the whole of humanity. I found myself feeling great admiration and yet pity for civilization and its subjects. The closeups of human faces juxtaposed with the immense intimidating nature of civilization and industry creates a remarkable sense of intimacy.
label: reaction

text: My absolute favorite sequence in Koyaanisqatsi is the Grid — the long portion with shots of people in the subway, conveyor belts, and sped-up shots of cars weaving through traffic. The way it gradually builds up intensity and finishes with just streaks of light is nothing short of awesome. Leaves a big grin on my face every time.
label: reaction

text: Just finished watching Koyaanisqatsi. Mind blowing film. I can still see images from the film pass through my head and the score will stick around for a while. Reminded me of Man With a Movie Camera — not only because of its shooting style but also because they both perfectly capture the world at the time they were filmed.
label: reaction

text: I think I like films that end abruptly. Movies are afraid to go out on a high note — they try to tie up loose ends which causes the ending to peter out. I often think while watching that a certain scene towards the end would've been the perfect ending but instead the film just overstays its welcome.
label: reaction

text: For an abrupt ending to work, the film needs a strong thesis and the gut-punch ending needs to be connected to that thesis. If it were just some random scene it would be bad filmmaking. But if the scene makes you go what was that all about and then in reflection makes more and more sense — the ending gets elevated immensely.
label: analysis

text: Nothing worse than a film you watch and feel would be much better if it ended a few scenes before. The third act is in general the most difficult. In general I take unusual or abrupt endings over insistently trying to close all open points like it was some kind of requirement.
label: hot_take

text: Thelma and Louise and Starman both cut to credits right at their emotional peaks without any denouement whatsoever. They're both so effective that I don't know why more movies don't try something similar.
label: hot_take

text: I saw Blair Witch Project on opening day in a packed arthouse theatre before any commercial hype. When it suddenly ended and the credits rolled the entire audience was in stunned silence for about 5 minutes before erupting in thunderous applause for 10 minutes. The staff had to come shoo us out because we were all so shocked and excited.
label: reaction

text: A lot of Haneke's films end at what seems like a weird place — sometimes abruptly with what feels like no conclusion to the plot. It gives his films an uneasy anxious feeling. There's a building of tension and curiosity that is deliberately not being released or satisfied. Just like how in real life you don't always get a clean simple resolution to things.
label: analysis

text: Antonioni's Blow Up ends incredibly suddenly and unexpectedly. After all that happens it's just such a strange seemingly random scene to end on — it forces you to consider its meaning. This final scene comes just after what seemed like it was leading to the climax, then the movie ends without any climax, which was almost subversive even way back then.
label: analysis

text: In My Dinner with Andre, Wally never spells it out to Andre: you are rich. Not everyone can go to Tibet, India, Sweden, and Scotland and host Buddhist monks within a few years. You can fly to India and complain about being a tourist where I consider taking a cab a treat. I'm really hoping you pay for dinner because I'm struggling to make rent.
label: hot_take

text: My Dinner with Andre is a pretty accurate depiction of the social dynamic you find among people in the arts — lots of working and middle-class artists struggling to make ends meet, and lots of rich artists who have never had to worry about rent. They are brought together by shared pursuits but have vastly different living situations and worldviews.
label: analysis

text: Both Wallace Shawn and André Gregory were upper middle class — Shawn's father was the editor of The New Yorker and both attended Harvard. So this isn't so much a direct class critique as it is an exploration of how people of different life experiences and philosophies interpret class and its role in society.
label: analysis

text: I can't think of another film I actively hated. I love films — all genres. This stands alone as painful time I will never get back. Even the worst movies have their charm. This was the most condescending tedious piece of shite I have ever seen. There is no point to it, it is charmless.
label: reaction

text: As a committed socialist Wallace Shawn certainly thinks a great deal about social class. He used the film to make a societal critique focused on the arbitrariness of the roles we play. His essay Why I Call Myself a Socialist sheds light on the choices made in the film.
label: analysis

text: Too many movies ignore money as a plot point. Friends is the classic example — how do these six 20-somethings afford spacious apartments in the most expensive city while serving part time at a coffee shop? You can pick any number of movies where the plot is undermined by the absence of money as a realistic constraint.
label: hot_take

text: Burning does the abrupt ending very well. There are so many loose threads in the best way possible — first time watching I really didn't see it coming. That ending stayed with me for months and still regularly pops up in my mind's eye.
label: reaction

text: I saw Koyaanisqatsi in a theater at its release and at the end when the lights came up we exchanged Holy F expressions. Many people in the theater were offended by the content — you could hear them huff and snort and become restless. Quite a few people got up and left during the film.
label: reaction

text: Cuaron had a huge stylistic shift with Y Tu Mama Tambien. Everything leading up to that had been decent but workmanlike. Y Tu saw Cuaron shift to a long-take oriented approach to mise en scene, very handheld camera work, and more naturalistic performances. He went from a fairly run-of-the-mill director to an auteur.
label: analysis

text: Fellini started out with the Neorealists making very grounded sedate films. Then he started moving the camera a little more in each film until by 8½ he was fully Felliniesque. He feels like the standard bearer for the abrupt stylistic shift that divides a career into two distinct halves.
label: analysis

text: Barry Lyndon's treatment of male honour is paradoxical — the opening duel is played for laughs to mock masculine pride, but Lord Bullington's arc reframes honour as something that can be both destructive and necessary. Kubrick isn't simply satirising these values; he's showing how they become tragic when misapplied.
label: analysis

text: I've long felt the same about Rosemary's Baby — its pacing, its sense of focus and tautness. Some films work like clockwork even on the tenth watch, scene to scene, line to line. Rosemary's Baby is interesting in that it doesn't seem to partake in genre clichés much for its structure but feels self-generating out of Polanski's sensibilities.
label: analysis

text: The Obsession review was packed with energy and enthusiasm — I literally jumped out of my seat three times. The lead performance is insane. Absolute must-see, go in blind. An eerie terrifying commentary on the world of dating and the consequences of wanting everything you think will make your life better.
label: reaction

text: I've always thought that genre cinema's relationship with the canon is a lot healthier than literature's. Noir is respected and is basically trashy crime novels adapted to the screen with elevated cinematography. Science fiction is much more accepted in the film canon than in literature.
label: analysis
